3:25 DYK CAP Schema Evolution

Transcription

00;00;00;17 - 00;00;05;25
Announcer
This is the developer news for July the 2nd, 2026.

00;00;05;28 - 00;00;09;23
Ajay Soreng
Hello everyone! June Developer Challenge is at an end and

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;30;02
Ajay Soreng
what a challenge it was. 20 questions, one for every working day of all. Full week of June on details from our documentation were eagerly answered by many, many participants. Well done to each and every one of you who took part. We hope you enjoyed digging into the cap documentation and learning various aspects along the way.

00;00;30;04 - 00;00;55;14
Ajay Soreng
Check out the Challenge Wrapper post for more details. This includes the analysis of the participation and the answers. And next up we have July's Developer Challenge on CAP status transition flows. We will together build a simple, fun game application using CAP. Due to the SAP CAP philosophy status transition flows demonstrate how you can model your state transitions while writing minimum code.

00;00;55;22 - 00;01;04;08
Ajay Soreng
I welcome you all to join the July SAP Developer Challenge. Linked to both the challenges will be in the description. Happy learning!

00;01;04;10 - 00;01;32;06
Witalij Rudnicki
The SAP HANA Cloud Q2 2026 release is now available, and I'd like to outline the key technical advancements. First, we are enabling a genetic data access. The SAP HANA Cloud now includes automated Knowledge Graph generation from your metadata, eliminating the need, some manual semantic modeling, and enabling AI driven data interactions. In addition, we will introduce identical tools for database object discovery and data retrieval.

00;01;32;13 - 00;02;04;03
Witalij Rudnicki
These tools allow you to query enterprise data using natural language, reducing the dependency on pSQL expertise. Did I just say that? Okay, the Knowledge Graph engine has been updated to support transforming knowledge graphs into property graphs, enabling advanced analytics. Additionally, SPARQL Federation queries are now available for instant access to distributed data across multiple environments. We have deepened our integration in SAP HANA Cloud with SAP AI Core.

00;02;04;06 - 00;02;28;08
Witalij Rudnicki
The new SQL predict function enables the use of tabular AI models like rapid one directly within the database, while AI text completion introduces LLM powered text generation into a scale. Workflows. For more details, join the upcoming in-depth webcast with the product managers or check the blog post on the SAP Community.

00;02;28;11 - 00;02;55;27
Nico Schoenteich
Hi everyone, and welcome to the SAP Developer News. AI is changing the way enterprise applications are built, used and experienced, and that raises fundamental questions on where SAP's UI technology is headed in the future. And we want to hear from you, which is why we've created a short anonymous survey to answer questions like how well does UI5 fit your needs in the age of AI?

00;02;55;27 - 00;03;21;29
Nico Schoenteich
And what kind of experiences do you see dominating user interfaces in the coming years? The survey takes around five minutes to complete, is completely anonymous, and it's relevant for anybody who's working with SAP UI technologies. Really. Whether you are a developer, architect or decision maker, make sure your voice is heard. Check out the link in the description. Hope to talk to you soon.

00;03;22;00 - 00;03;28;08
Nico Schoenteich
Bye.

00;03;28;10 - 00;03;54;24
Ajay Soreng
Did you know that Cloud Application Programing Model is a database agnostic framework? Yes, indeed. And did you also know that CAP supports schema evolution? So what is it? This is a tiny sample CAP application with persistent SQL database in order to change the data model. You can either add a new field to an existing entity, or you can make compatible changes to the existing fields in an entity.

00;03;54;28 - 00;04;15;03
Ajay Soreng
In this particular example, I'm adding a new field called a specialist to the books entity, and all you have to do is to again deploy your model again and you are good to go. And we see that there is no more data loss and no more data migration nightmares. Happy coding.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and AI: Telling the story to drive adoption
entry.content:
AI is moving fast. Customers are asking bigger questions. Are their data foundations ready?

Join this webinar and learn how to answer with confidence.

We'll break down how SAP Business Data Cloud creates a governed business data fabric, connects SAP and non-SAP data, and delivers trusted AI insights through SAP Cloud ERP Intelligence and Finance Intelligence. You'll also see native SAP Databricks integration in action, including zero-copy delta sharing and machine learning scenarios.

After that, we'll shift from technology to opportunity. Learn how to package partner services, extend SAP data products, support business warehouse modernization, and prepare customers for what's coming next, including Joule, SAP Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and intelligent business applications.

If you're looking for practical messaging, technical understanding, and new ways to create customer value, this session is for you.

Save your seat and register now. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/jan/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012794.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPBusinessDataCloud #AIInnovation #SAPDatabricks #BusinessTransformation #DataDrivenAI

entry.title: What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud – July 2026
entry.content:
The Q2 2026 release of SAP HANA Cloud continues to advance our vision of delivering an intelligent, AI-native, and enterprise-scale data platform. This quarter introduces innovations that make it easier to build AI-powered applications, manage large-scale enterprise workloads, simplify database administration, and strengthen security across your data landscape.

From deeper integration with SAP AI Core and new machine learning capabilities, to larger database instance sizes, enhanced data integration, and intelligent administration tools, this release allows you to unlock greater value from your data while reducing operational complexity.

Whether you're building next-generation AI applications, modernizing your enterprise data landscape, or optimizing business-critical workloads, SAP HANA Cloud continues to provide the foundation for secure, scalable, and intelligent data management.

Let's take a look at some of the key innovations introduced with the Q2 2026 release.

 

Agentic Data Access in SAP HANA Cloud

We're taking another major step toward an AI-native data platform with new capabilities that enable agentic data access in SAP HANA Cloud.

At the core of this innovation is the ability to automatically generate a custom database objects knowledge graph. SAP HANA Cloud scans your database metadata including: tables, views, columns, and their relationships, and automatically builds a semantic representation of your data landscape.

By eliminating the need for manual semantic modeling, this knowledge graph provides a rich foundation for data discovery, reasoning, and AI-driven interactions. It enables both users and AI agents to better understand how enterprise data is organized and connected.

Building on this semantic foundation, we are introducing the Database Object Discovery Tool and the Data Retrieval Tool, available beginning in early July.

Today, working with enterprise data often requires you to understand complex database schemas, identify the right database objects, and manually write SQL queries to retrieve information. With these new capabilities, you can simply describe what they are looking for using natural language.

The Database Object Discovery Tool identifies the relevant database objects and relationships by leveraging the database objects knowledge graph. The Data Retrieval Tool then translates your request into an executable SQL statement and runs it directly within SAP HANA Cloud.

Together, these capabilities dramatically simplify the way we can discover, understand, and query enterprise data, without requiring deep SQL expertise or detailed knowledge of the underlying database schema.

Built on the multi-model foundation and Knowledge Graph Engine of SAP HANA Cloud, these innovations lay the groundwork for a new generation of conversational, agent-driven, and AI-powered data experiences, making it easier than ever to build intelligent applications on enterprise data.

To learn more about the agentic database tools, take a look at Shabana's blog post.

 

Natural Language Processing (NLP), Machine Learning and AI Core integration 

With the Q2 2026 release of SAP HANA Cloud, we continue to strengthen embedded NLP and Machine Learning capabilities with broader SAP AI Core integration, unlocking access to SAP RPT-1 and  LLMs and thus completely new type of AI-driven database applications. 

Key highlights as part of the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) include: 

  • A new function for synthetic data generation using diffusion models, e.g for securing data privacy when training custom AI models 
  • Time Series Forecasting function enhancements like support for lagged regressors with Additive Model Time Series Analysis (aka prophet) and easier time series cross validation using the Unified Time Series procedures for better forecast predictions 
  • Further enhancements include columnar analysis of large data sets using Online Univariate Analysis 

The full list of PAL enhancements for SAP HANA Cloud 2026 Q2 is documented here. Additionally, the python Machine Learning client for SAP HANA 2.29 is supporting several new functions (see here) like PCA-based vector dimension reduction or the text tf-idf operator in AutoML. 

Key highlights for SAP AI Core integration include: 

  • Remote sources to SAP AI Core now supporting use of LLMs and SAP RPT-1 as standardized and secure gateway for 3rd party LLMs access 
  • Benefit from on-the-fly predictions, without the need to create, train and manage use-case specific ML models by simply utilizing the new AI_TABULAR_PREDICTION SQL procedure to access tabular AI models like SAP RPT-1  
  • Ability to leverage LLM capabilities, directly from the database SQL processing engine, by simply utilizing the new AI_TEXT_COMPLETION SQL function to execute one-shot-tasks like text generation or text summarization based on your text data managed in SAP HANA Cloud, unlocking for completely new type of LLM-driven application scenarios. 

These enhancements focus on making tabular AI predictions using custom AI models or semantic data retrieval scenario easier to use and applied to real business application data. 

 

 

Right-sizing your instance with Performance Class Advisor

Knowing whether your SAP HANA Cloud instance is the right size shouldn't require manual analysis or reactive firefighting. The Performance Class Advisor changes that by continuously monitoring your instance's compute and memory usage and automatically generating right-sizing recommendations every Sunday, without any manual trigger.

You choose a lookback window of 2, 4, or 6 weeks to match your workload patterns, and the advisor checks whether CPU or memory usage has exceeded 80% during that period, if either threshold is breached, a recommendation is triggered. The advisor then calculates the optimal upsized spec, targeting approximately 20% additional capacity and snapping to the nearest valid instance configuration.

In this initial release, recommendations are limited to upsizing only, as downsizing carries higher risk given that short lookback windows may not capture infrequent but critical workload spikes, and that is in our backlog to investigate further. The benefits are threefold: right-sizing guidance based on actual historical workload patterns rather than guesswork; proactive resource management that surfaces capacity pressure before it impacts business continuity, giving you time to act rather than react; and automated weekly insights with zero manual effort — switch it on once, and stay informed from there. 

 

Automated SQL Plan Management with SQL Plan Advisor 

SQL execution performance can fluctuate for various reasons. A common culprit is a change in the execution plan within the SQL Plan Cache, either because a cached plan becomes suboptimal over time, or because an inefficient plan was cached based on the initial parameter sets used during compilation. 

To better manage these regressions, SQL Plan Advisor was introduced in QRC 2/2024. It automatically detects degraded query executions by analyzing historical performance statistics stored via SQL Plan Stability. To resolve a performance dip, older, historical plans had to be verified to ensure they performed faster than the currently cached plan. However, this verification and testing process could only be triggered manually. 

With QRC 2/2026, this testing process is now fully automated. The system autonomously executes faster historical plans to verify their effectiveness, swapping out the degraded cached plan to seamlessly restore optimal database performance. 

Please check out the details in the blog by @Taesuk  found here. 

 

Database Administration & Developer Productivity

Import & Export  

SAP HANA Cloud Central will soon be adding a new top-level application named Import and Export designed to enable users to quickly complete import and export operations within SAP HANA Cloud. This application provides guided wizards for importing data into a table, exporting data from a table or view, and importing and exporting both catalog objects and SAP HANA deployment infrastructure containers.  Supported storage providers include SAP HANA Cloud data lake, Amazon S3, Microsoft Azure storage, and Google Cloud storage. Users can also view the generated SQL for any import or export task directly within the wizard. 

 Additionally, the export wizard will support the ability to provide a query to further filter the data being exported. 

 

SQL Console 

Improved Tab Management 
Previously if a SQL console tab was closed accidently, its contents were gone. Users can now restore closed tabs using the Restore Last Closed Tab option in the toolbar. Additionally, tabs can be renamed and reordered, making it easier to stay organized when working across multiple queries at once.  

A new search capability is also available from the actions menu, that lets users search across the content of all open tabs enabling a specific query to be quickly located.  

Enhanced Syntax Help Panel  
The syntax help panel has also received a refresh. Object details are now collapsible, reducing visual clutter when users only need a quick reference. Columns and parameters are also searchable, which allows users to find what they need without scrolling through extensive lists. These columns are now clickable as well – selecting one directly inserts it into the editor.  

SQL Results  
Users now also have more control within SQL results, which has been enhanced with options to handle how data is displayed and refreshed. Users can customize which columns they want to be shown or hidden and also reorder them in whichever way that suits their workflow. Results can also be re-executed on demand without requiring parameters to be re-entered.  

 

Data Integration (Federation & Replication)

SQL on Files: Apache Iceberg REST Catalog support in SAP HANA Cloud 

Direct read-only access to Apache Iceberg tables in object storage, which has been introduced since QRC 3/2025, has offered real-time analytics on Iceberg lakehouses without moving data into SAP HANA Cloud. With QRC 2/2026, it evolves further by supporting Apache Iceberg REST Catalogs, which is how the modern enterprise lakehouses centralize table management, access control, and metadata governance. 

Through the new icebergcatalog adapter, SAP HANA Cloud now connects directly to spec-compliant Iceberg REST Catalogs across AWS, Azure, and GCP, bringing enterprise-grade integration to your Iceberg lakehouse with minimal effort. Note that while the adapter is designed to work with any spec-compliant Iceberg REST Catalog, only a defined set of providers is officially certified by SAP per QRC. 

To learn more about SAP HANA Cloud's new Apache Iceberg REST Catalog support, take a look at @SeungjoonLee's blog post here. 

S/4HANA CDS View Entity replication to SAP HANA Cloud 

S/4HANA Federated access was introduced in QRC 1/2024, as detailed in the blog Taking Data Federation to the Next Level: Accessing Remote ABAP CDS View Entities in SAP HANA Cloud. Building on this, QRC 2/2026 introduces support for replicating data directly to SAP HANA Cloud. 

CDS view replication allows you to copy data from SAP S/4HANA CDS view entities into local tables within SAP HANA Cloud, enabling fast local analytics without the need for remote federated queries.

Key benefits include: 

  • Lower Latency: Data resides locally, and efficiency is maximized by replicating only changed rows. 
  • Reduced Source Overhead: Impact on the source system is minimized because replication can be scheduled at custom intervals or run entirely on demand. 
  • Flexible Filtering: Data can be filtered by specific rows, selected columns, or both. 

You can configure replication via SQL or by using the Data Replication tool in SAP HANA Cloud Central. 

To learn more about SAP HANA Cloud’s new S/4HANA CDS View replication, check out @Taesuk ’s blog post here. 

 

Security, Data Protection & Privacy

Post-quantum cryptography (PQC) for Transport Layer Security (TLS) connections 

With the Q2 2026 release, SAP HANA Cloud database introduces post-quantum cryptography (PQC) for TLS connections, providing quantum-resistant protection for all data in transit between clients and SAP HANA Cloud databases. 

Post-quantum cryptography addresses the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat — where adversaries capture encrypted traffic today with the intent of decrypting it once quantum computers become capable. By enabling PQC now, SAP HANA Cloud ensures that data encrypted today remains protected against future quantum attacks. 

Key capabilities include: 

Hybrid Post-Quantum Key Exchange 

A transparent ML-KEM key establishment negotiated during TLS 1.3 handshakes, combining classical and quantum-resistant algorithms. Enabled by default for all SAP HANA Cloud databases with no customer configuration required. 

Full Backward Compatibility 

Clients with PQC support (SAP Client using OpenSSL 3.5+, SAP HANA client 2.29+) automatically negotiate the quantum-resistant hybrid exchange. Clients without PQC support safely fall back to classical key exchange with zero disruption to existing applications. 

Built-in Observability 

New SSL_KEY_EXCHANGE and SSL_SIGNATURE columns in M_CONNECTIONS and M_OUTBOUND_NETWORK_IO system views, enabling administrators to verify PQC adoption per connection and identify clients that need upgrading. 

The feature is aligned with NIST post-quantum standards (ML-KEM / FIPS 203) ahead of anticipated regulatory mandates and requires no customer action to activate. 

Typical use cases include verifying quantum-safe connection status across the landscape and meeting compliance requirements for post-quantum readiness. 

 

Access Logging & Column-Level Security for SQL Views

SAP HANA Cloud introduces two complementary capabilities for protecting sensitive data: access logging for columns containing sensitive data and column-level security for SQL views. Together, these address two sides of the compliance equation - preventing unauthorized access and detecting all access for audit purposes - enabling compliant direct SQL access to data containing sensitive columns. 

The Context 

Regulations like GDPR require both prevention of unauthorized access to personal data AND demonstrable accountability for all access. Direct SQL access to HANA databases containing sensitive data requires enforcement and audit trails to meet these compliance requirements. 

These two capabilities address this: 

Prevent: 

Column-Level Security for SQL Views 

Ensures unauthorized users cannot see sensitive columns (returned as NULL) 

Detect: 

Access Logging for Sensitive Columns 

Logs who accessed which sensitive columns, when, and on which table (values never logged) 

Access Logging 

Access logging is activated directly in the table definition using the AUDIT (READ | NONE) syntax. Once columns are marked, read access is automatically reported via the predefined "SAP - column access" audit policy, as no custom policy creation is needed. Both direct and indirect access is reported, including access via views or during procedure execution. The audit log entry includes the schema name, table name, and the list of accessed columns (quoted and comma-separated), while the actual data values and SQL statements are never logged - ensuring the audit trail provides accountability without creating a secondary repository of sensitive data. 

Column-Level Security 

Column-level security for SQL views provides a dynamic access control mechanism. Columns on SQL views are tagged with DPP classification identifying Sensitive Personal Data, and the authorization model determines which tagged columns are permitted for access. At query execution time, the engine evaluates authorization dynamically – this evaluation is not tied to the current database user but can rely on any information in the session context or other tables. Authorized columns return actual values, while unauthorized columns return NULL. The key design decision is that columns are masked, not omitted - query structure remains stable for all consumers, meaning downstream dashboards, pipelines, and applications continue to function without modification. 

Combined Value 

Together, these capabilities enable compliant direct SQL access to sensitive data in SAP HANA Cloud. Column-level security ensures users see only data they are authorized to access, while access logging provides auditable evidence of all access events, satisfying both the prevention and accountability requirements of GDPR and similar regulations.
The operational overhead is minimal: DDL-based activation for access logging, a system-managed audit policy, and dynamic authorization evaluation for column-level security - with no duplicate views, no application-layer workarounds, and no custom configuration frameworks required. 

 

Increase in-memory capacity with up to 16 TB and 24 TB Instances on AWS 

As enterprise workloads continue to grow in size and complexity, organizations increasingly require larger in-memory database configurations to support mission-critical transactional and analytical applications.
With the Q2 2026 release, SAP HANA Cloud expands its scalability by introducing support for 16 TB and 24 TB database instances on AWS.

These new instance sizes are designed for customers running some of the most demanding enterprise workloads, providing the processing power and memory capacity required for large-scale SAP and custom applications while maintaining the simplicity of a fully managed cloud service.

To ensure predictable performance and guaranteed resource availability, these instances are deployed on dedicated, pre-reserved physical infrastructure that is allocated exclusively to a customer's subaccount within a selected AWS availability zone. The reserved capacity remains available throughout the three-year reservation period, regardless of whether the database instance is running, stopped, paused, or temporarily deprovisioned.

Customers can request these instance sizes directly through SAP HANA Cloud Central by opening an SAP support ticket. Once the requested capacity has been approved and the dedicated infrastructure has been provisioned, the new database instance can be deployed against the reserved resources.

Support for 16 TB and 24 TB instances is currently available in selected AWS regions. For the latest information on supported regions and availability zones, please refer to the SAP HANA Cloud documentation.

 

Data Lake Innovations

Thin Clone Decoupling for Data Lake Relational Engine 

Building on the thin clone capability in SAP HANA Cloud, data lake relational engine, which is designed for creation and deployment of clones of petabyte-scale databases in development and testing environments in just a few minutes, we are introducing thin clone decoupling. Thin clone decoupling promotes a thin clone to a fully standalone SAP HANA Cloud, data lake relational engine instance. Once the decoupling process completes, the instance operates independently, completely detached from the parent instance. 

Upon decoupling, backups are enabled on the decoupled instance, making it eligible for disaster recovery scenarios. The decoupled instance can itself serve as a source for creating new thin clones. 

 Thin clone decoupling opens a clear path for promoting development or test databases to production-grade instances improving data reliability and integrity. 

 

 

Thanks for taking the time to explore the What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud on Q2 2026 innovations!

If you’d like to go deeper into the technical details of the Q2 2026 release, we recommend visiting the What’s New Viewer in the technical documentation. It provides a comprehensive overview of the full release scope. To stay informed about upcoming innovations, announcements, and best practices, follow the SAP HANA Cloud tag.

You can also find our latest blog posts by searching for the #whatsnewinsaphanacloud hashtag.

If you missed earlier What’s New webinars, you’ll find recordings of past sessions and upcoming events in our YouTube playlist. Our What’s New webinar covering the Q2 2026 innovations will be posted on this playlist very soon. You can also download the slides from the webinar here.

Do you have questions about SAP HANA Cloud or want to share your thoughts on the new features?
Join the conversation in the SAP HANA Cloud Community Q&A or leave a comment below. We’d love to hear from you!

entry.title: Support Accreditation Unit 2 Lesson: Resolving Issues with Troubleshooting and Diagnostic Tools
entry.content:
This blog provides concise details on various troubleshooting and diagnostics tools to enable you to solve challenges across your SAP landscape. This is an excerpt from the Support Accreditation learning course Unit 2 Lesson 2. The tools we will deep-dive into are:

  • Support Log Assistant
  • Automated Search for SAP Notes
  • Performance Assistant for SAP Notes and SAP Knowledge Base Articles Search
  • Kernel Snapshot Analyzer
  • SAP BusinessObjects BI Support Tool
  • Flexible Log Reader

Ready to level up your SAP support journey? Get Support Accredited and unlock the full power of self-service tools, expert tips, and multi-channels. Earn an SAP-certified digital badge to showcase your expertise.  Begin your Support Accreditation journey today!

 

Support Log Assistant

Overview

  • The support log assistant tool analyses text files such as logs, traces, and configuration files automatically for known issues and recommendations.
  • On the SAP for Me customer portal, the support log assistant analysis is activated during case creation, when troubleshooting files are attached.
  • It is also freely available as a standalone self-service web application, powered by the SAP Business Technology Platform, scan log/trace files in your web browser.
  • Identifies log issues and provides references to relevant SAP Notes and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

Benefits

  • 24/7 automated, expert-level log file analysis.
  • Accelerated issue resolution without the need to open a support case.
  • Save time by reducing manual log analysis and solution searches.
  • Generates a PDF report of log analysis findings and recommendations.
  • Easily share results with peers to extend best practices and lessons learned.

Access

When you create a case and attach files to it in the "Attachments" section, support log assistant analysis occurs automatically. You can also access the support log assistant as a standalone tool by either:

  • Choosing the "Services & Support" dashboard in SAP for Me.
  • Using this link to connect directly to the standalone tool.

 

You can then use the support log assistant effectively by following the steps below:

  1. Add log files.

     

  2. To scan files, choose Next.

     

  3. Review raised alerts and suggested solutions.

     

  4. Provide feedback on the efficacy of the analysis.

     

  5. Download the "Analysis Report" and "Archive Session Files" if you want to save or share the analysis results.

     

Relevant Links

  • Support log assistant standalone, self-service tool
  • Support log assistant video demo
  • SAP Knowledge Base Article: 2838708 - Using the support log assistant to automate support-related file analysis
  • SAP Help Portal: Support log assistant Wiki
  • List of File Types that can be Analyzed

 

Automated Search for SAP Notes

Overview

  • The Automated Notes Search Tool (ANST) simplifies the identification of SAP correction notes for functional issues, customer codes, and customizing tables, particularly for standard processes.
  • To access SAP-relevant notes and identify issues related to customer code and customizing tables, users can initiate the process by starting the ANST transaction.
  • Additional resources are available for further understanding, including FAQs, instructional videos, and the SAP Help Portal.
  • Explore the following blogs for more insights: ANST and What is ANST?

Benefits

  • The tool only identifies SAP Notes that are relevant to the specific issue, streamlining the search process.
  • Unlike traditional note searches that require specific search terms, this tool automatically searches for SAP Notes based on the ABAP objects involved in the process, simplifying the search.
  • The tool is designed for ABAP-based systems, whether they run directly in the back-end system or through a front end, such as SAP Fiori or portals.

Access

  • Start an ANST transaction from the back-end and reproduce the issue. You can find more information in SAP Knowledge Base Article 1818192.
  • Before you proceed, make sure that the following notes is implemented in your system:2286869. Probably, the note has been applied by support package but manual steps have to be followed anyway.

 

 

Performance Assistant for SAP Notes and SAP Knowledge Base Articles Search

Overview

  • The Performance Assistant simplifies the search for SAP Notes and SAP Knowledge Base Articles related to ABAP messages. It's based on the relevant message code, including the message class and number.
  • For additional information, you can check the FAQ, watch an informative video, or access in-depth details in the blog.

Benefits

  • Receive real-time updates for documentation.
  • The Performance Assistant instantly identifies new SAP Notes containing the relevant ABAP message code, ensuring you benefit from immediate updates and information.

Access

  • When you encounter an error message, double-click the error message and, to proceed with additional actions, choose the binoculars or search icon.
  • For more detailed guidance on this process, you can find additional information in SAP Knowledge Base Article 2096401.

 

 

Kernel Snapshot Analyzer

Overview

  • Server snapshots can be triggered automatically or manually.
  • The kernel snapshot analyzer runs over 36 analyzers for different scenarios.
  • Read a blog introducing kernel snapshot and kernel snapshot analyzer on SAP Community.
  • SAP Knowledge Base Article 3110588 explains how the kernel snapshot analyzer helps analyze system-wide issues and provides solutions using server snapshots.

Benefits

  • The tool gathers the most important trace files used for troubleshooting.
  • It streamlines the analysis of ABAP system-wide problems, such as system hang-ups, slow performance, resource bottlenecks, and high business load situations.
  • Additionally, it visualizes errors and provides solutions along with configuration recommendations.

Access

  • A Java-based application is available for download from SAP Knowledge Base Article 3110588.
  • SAP Knowledge Base Article 3122914 provides a step-by-step guide to collecting a server snapshot and analyzing it with the kernel snapshot analyzer.

 

 

SAP BusinessObjects BI Support Tool

Overview

  • The SAP BusinessObjects BI support tool is a simple and easy-to-deploy client for the SAP BusinessObjects BI platform.
  • Its landscape analysis report offers a comprehensive view of the configuration and topology of SAP Business Objects Business Intelligence.
  • The tool automatically checks for SAP Best Practices, system configuration, service distribution, and other alerts.
  • It includes a toolbox with various root cause analysis and administrative tools, making it a lightweight supportability solution that simplifies the work of BI administrators.

Benefits

  • It eliminates question ping-pong and speeds up issue resolution.
  • Historical system configuration capture and change analysis are supported.
  • Admins save time with built-in tools, such as security analyser, authentication, and trace wizards.
  • Proactive issue identification is enabled through customizable landscape alerts.
  • Sensitive report data can be secured for enhanced security.

Access

  • The tool is free for all SAP BusinessObjects customers and can be downloaded via the Software Download Center.
  • Additional information and resources can be found on the SAP Help Portal.
  • There is a version 2.1 webinar and demo available for further insights and demonstrations.

 

 

Flexible Log Reader

Overview

  • A versatile log reading application for analyzing text log files, regardless of their source application
  • Designed for SAP BI platform trace logs (GLFs) and other text formats
  • Features powerful search and filtering options
  • Includes configuration files and expert-created plugins that facilitate automatic analysis and tagging for quick access to critical information
  • A versatile log reading application for analyzing text log files, regardless of their source application

Benefits

  • Uses pre-built categories for quick identification of common SAP BusinessObjects BI log messages.
  • Can efficiently read long chains of multiple logs with minimal memory usage.
  • Capable of opening and scanning large logs, often exceeding the available memory on the machine. For example, a 4GB machine can load approximately 18GB of logs.

Access

  • No installation is required. Download and extract the ZIP file.
  • Run the tool by double-clicking 'FlexiLogReader64.exe'.
  • You can download it from Flexible Log Reader on the SAP Help Portal.

 

Relevant Links

  • SAP KBA 1818192: FAQ for Automated Note Search Tool (ANST)
  • SAP KBA 2096401: FAQ for PANKS
  • SAP KBA 3110588: Kernel snapshot analyzer
  • SAP KBA 2138275: How to generate a landscape analysis report
  • SAP KBA 2203047: How to read and analyze log files

 

Thanks to all the experts from Global Customer Support for putting this content together!

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

How Utilities Build a Unified, Governed, Real‑Time Data Layer for Grid + Customer Operations

1.      Introduction

For SAP-centric utilities, the challenge is no longer simply managing growing volumes of data. Utilities have invested heavily in SAP IS-U, SAP S/4HANA, AMI platforms, SCADA systems, GIS applications, outage management solutions, and a growing ecosystem of grid technologies. Yet much of this information remains trapped within operational silos, making it difficult to create a unified view of grid and customer operations.

The real problem is no longer collecting data, but connecting, governing, and using it across the enterprise.

As utilities navigate the rise of distributed energy resources (DERs), electric vehicles (EVs), advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), and increasingly dynamic grid operations, traditional batch-oriented architectures struggle to keep pace. The grid is becoming real time, but the data architecture often remains fragmented.

A Business Data Fabric built on SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud provides a modern architectural approach for unifying SAP and non-SAP data without discarding existing investments. By combining data integration, semantic modeling, governance, and real-time access capabilities, utilities can create a trusted enterprise data layer that supports analytics, AI, operational intelligence, and regulatory reporting from a common foundation.

This is where Utility Data Fabric becomes essential—and where SAP BTP provides the platform to make it practical.

Most utilities still operate with:

  • Siloed operational systems
  • Fragmented data marts
  • Batch‑driven ETL pipelines
  • Inconsistent semantic definitions
  • Limited real‑time visibility

This creates a fundamental gap: The grid is real‑time, but the data architecture is not.

This is where Utility Data Fabric becomes essential — and SAP BTP provides the platform to build it.

2.      A Utility Data Fabric in Action: Transformer Outage Scenario

To understand how Utility Data Fabric works, consider a common operational event transformer failure affecting several hundred customers.

A SCADA system detects abnormal voltage conditions and reports the event in real time. AMI smart meters simultaneously stop communicating, confirming the outage footprint. GIS identifies the affected transformer, feeder, and service territory, while the Outage Management System (OMS) creates an outage event and initiates restoration activities.

Within the Utility Data Fabric, these events are combined with asset history, maintenance records, weather conditions, and customer information to create a unified operational view. Customer notification systems can automatically communicate outage information, analytics platforms can assess business impact, and AI models can predict restoration timelines based on historical outage patterns.

This single event touches nearly every layer of the Data Fabric—from data ingestion and semantic modeling to governance, analytics, and AI-driven decision support.

3.      What Utility Data Fabric Actually Means

Data Fabric is not a single product. It is an architectural approach that unifies:

  • Data ingestion
  • Data storage
  • Semantic modeling
  • Governance
  • Access control
  • Analytics
  • AI/ML consumption

Across all utility domains:

  • Grid operations
  • Customer operations
  • Metering
  • Billing
  • Asset management
  • Field operations
  • Regulatory reporting

In simple terms: A Data Fabric makes data findable, usable, trusted, and real‑time — across the entire utility.

SAP BTP provides the building blocks to make this practical.

4.      The Core Components of a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

Data Lake Layer

This is the foundation. SAP HANA Cloud provides the underlying multi-model data platform for storing and processing enterprise data, while SAP Datasphere delivers the semantic, integration, and governance capabilities that form the Business Data Fabric layer. A key principle of SAP's Business Data Fabric approach is that not all data must be physically moved into a central repository. SAP Datasphere supports both replication and federation techniques, allowing utilities to access and combine data across SAP and non-SAP systems while minimizing unnecessary data duplication. This approach helps reduce integration costs, improve data freshness, and preserve investments in existing operational platforms. For large historical datasets such as AMI interval reads and telemetry archives, SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake can provide lower-cost storage while maintaining integration with Datasphere and HANA Cloud analytics services. In the transformer outage scenario, telemetry from SCADA, outage events from OMS, AMI meter status, GIS network topology, and customer information are ingested and made available through the Data Fabric for unified analysis

The Data Fabric approach allows utilities to federate data across hybrid landscapes—keeping legacy data in on-premises systems while virtualizing access in the cloud—reducing the latency and cost of moving massive, static datasets.

Utilities ingest:

  • AMI meter events
  • SCADA/IoT streams
  • DER telemetry
  • Weather + Wildfire Risk Data
  • Customer interactions
  • Billing determinants
  • Asset health and maintenance history
  • Outage management events (OMS)

For utilities, geospatial information is as important as transactional data. Modern Data Fabrics must integrate GIS systems, network topology models, feeder maps, outage polygons, vegetation management zones, and wildfire risk areas. By combining spatial context with customer, asset, and operational data, utilities can perform advanced use cases such as outage impact analysis, restoration prioritization, vegetation risk assessment, DER hosting capacity planning, and climate resilience modeling. SAP Datasphere can combine this geospatial context with operational and customer data to create business-ready models for planning, analytics, and AI use cases.

  • The data lake must support:
  • High‑volume ingestion
  • Low‑cost storage
  • Real‑time streaming
  • Time‑series analytics
  • Geospatial analysis

This is where grid + customer data finally live together.

Analytical Model and Consumption Model concepts in Datasphere. In SAP Datasphere, analytical models define measures and semantics, while consumption models expose business‑ready views for analytics and AI. The goal is not to move every dataset into one database. The goal is to provide one trusted view of enterprise data regardless of where it physically resides

Semantic Models

This is the most misunderstood part of a Data Fabric.

Semantic models define:

  • What is a meter?
  • What is an outage?
  • What is a transformer?
  • What is billing determinant?
  • What is a customer event?

Without semantic consistency:

  • Analytics break
  • AI models fail
  • Reports contradict each other
  • Regulatory submissions become risky

A Business Data Fabric combines data integration, governance, semantic modeling, and real-time access into a single enterprise architecture without requiring all data to be physically centralized. SAP Datasphere’s Business Data Fabric capabilities allow utilities to create:

  • Harmonized data models
  • Domain‑specific views
  • Reusable data products
  • Cross‑domain relationships.

Rather than exposing raw tables or application-specific structures, utilities can publish governed data products that package data with business meaning, ownership, quality standards, and access controls. Examples include a customer 360 product, Asset Health product, Outage Operations product, DER Portfolio product, or Transformer Risk product. These reusable data products become the primary mechanism through which analytics teams, AI solutions, operational applications, and business users consume trusted enterprise data. For example, during a transformer outage, different systems may refer to the same asset using different identifiers. Semantic models help establish a consistent definition of transformers, feeders, outages, and affected customers across all participating systems.

Master Data Foundation

Semantic consistency begins with trusted master data. Utilities often maintain customer, asset, feeder, transformer, meter, and service point information across multiple systems, including SAP S/4HANA, SAP IS-U, GIS platforms, outage management systems, and third-party applications.

Utility Data Fabric must establish a consistent master data foundation so that the same business object is represented identically across the enterprise. Without master data harmonization, analytics become unreliable, AI models generate inconsistent results, and cross-domain reporting loses credibility.

By combining semantic models with governed master data, utilities create a trusted foundation for enterprise-wide analytics, operational intelligence, and regulatory reporting. SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) can further support this foundation by governing critical utility master data domains and synchronizing trust records across enterprise applications.

Governance Layer

Governance is not documentation. It is control + trust: Utilities must define

  • Data ownership (domain‑driven)
  • Data quality rules
  • Lineage and traceability
  • Access policies
  • Retention and compliance
  • Security and privacy

SAP BTP provides:

  • Central governance via Datasphere
  • Cataloging and lineage
  • Role‑based access
  • Policy enforcement
  • Auditability

Governance is what makes Data Fabric safe. Utility Data Fabrics must also address cybersecurity and regulatory requirements. As operational technology (OT) data from AMI, SCADA, and grid systems becomes integrated with enterprise IT platforms, utilities must enforce appropriate segmentation, access controls, auditability, and compliance requirements such as NERC CIP where applicable. During an outage investigation, data lineage and auditability help utilities understand where information originated, how it was transformed, and whether operational decisions were based on trusted data sources.

Active Metadata: The Intelligence Behind the Data Fabric

A modern Data Fabric does not operate solely on data; it also depends on metadata that describes how data is created, transformed, consumed, and governed. Active metadata captures information such as data lineage, ownership, quality scores, usage patterns, business definitions, and relationships between datasets.

Within SAP Datasphere, metadata helps utilities understand where data originated, who owns it, how it is being used, and whether it meets governance standards. As utility data landscapes continue to grow, active metadata becomes essential for automated impact analysis, policy enforcement, data discovery, and trusted AI consumption.

In many successful Data Fabric implementations, metadata acts as the operational intelligence layer that continuously monitors and improves the health of the overall data ecosystem.

Analytics for Grid + Customer

This is where Data Fabric becomes valuable. Utilities can build:

Grid Analytics

  • Outage prediction
  • Load forecasting
  • DER hosting capacity
  • Feeder‑level performance
  • Voltage optimization
  • Asset failure prediction

Customer Analytics

  • High‑bill alerts
  • Usage pattern segmentation
  • EV charging behavior
  • Payment risk scoring
  • Real‑time notifications
  • Personalized rate recommendations

Enterprise Analytics

  • Regulatory reporting
  • Operational KPIs
  • Financial performance
  • Workforce optimization

SAP Analytics Cloud + Datasphere + HANA Cloud provide a unified analytics stack.

AI Readiness Through Data Fabric

The long-term value of a Utility Data Fabric extends beyond reporting and analytics. It provides the trusted data foundation required for enterprise AI, machine learning, and autonomous decision-making. AI models perform best when they are trained and operated on governed, semantically consistent, and high-quality data.

By combining semantic models, master data harmonization, governance controls, and lineage capabilities, a Utility Data Fabric reduces the risk of inaccurate predictions, inconsistent recommendations, and model drift. This foundation becomes critical for initiatives such as outage prediction, asset failure forecasting, energy demand optimization, and intelligent customer engagement. Examples include outage prediction, transformer failure forecasting, DER optimization, and proactive customer notification models. In the transformer outage example, AI models can combine outage history, asset condition, weather data, and crew availability to improve restoration estimates and recommend remediation actions.

BTP Integration:

This architecture doesn't exist in a vacuum. It integrates directly with SAP Build Process Automation (for triggering workflows from data insights) and SAP AI Core (for deploying models trained on Datasphere data products). SAP Integration Suite plays a critical role in Data Fabric architecture by connecting SAP IS-U, SAP S/4HANA, SAP EAM, GIS systems, outage management platforms, and external data providers. Through Cloud Integration, API Management, and Event Mesh, Integration Suite enables secure, governed, and scalable movement of data across hybrid utility landscapes.

How the Data Fabric Works End‑to‑End

Step 1 — Ingest

  • AMI events via Event Mesh
  • IoT/SCADA via MQTT
  • Customer interactions via APIs
  • Asset data via S/4HANA

Traditional data architectures rely on batch-based movement of information between systems. Modern utility operations require a different approach. Smart meters, distributed energy resources, grid sensors, and customer engagement platforms continuously generate events that must be processed in near real time. SAP Event Mesh enables these events to be distributed across operational systems and data platforms, where they can be consumed by analytics, automation services, and AI models. creating a continuously updated Data Fabric rather than a periodically refreshed repository.

Step 2 — Store

  • Raw zone (immutable)
  • Refined zone (cleaned)
  • Curated zone (business‑ready)

Step 3 — Model

  • Semantic definitions
  • Domain views
  • Data products

Step 4 — Govern

  • Quality rules
  • Lineage
  • Access control

Step 5 — Consume

  • Grid operations
  • Customer operations
  • Field mobility
  • AI/ML models
  • Analytics dashboards

This is the Utility Data Fabric lifecycle.  The Data Fabric helps align grid‑level latency requirements (millisecond responses for protection) with enterprise‑level latency (near real‑time analytics), ensuring the data architecture does not become a bottleneck for critical operations.

Returning to our transformer outage example, the same operational event moves through every stage of the Data Fabric lifecycle—from ingestion and storage through semantic modeling, governance, analytics, and AI-driven decision support.

5.       Why Utilities Need Data Fabric Now

  • Because the grid is becoming real‑time: DERs, EVs, and AMI require continuous intelligence.
  • Because AI depends on clean, governed data: AI is useless without trusted data.
  • Because regulators expect transparency: Lineage, auditability, and data quality are no longer optional.
  • Because customer expectations have changed: Real‑time notifications, usage insights, and personalized experiences require unified data.
  • Because operational silos are too expensive: A Data Fabric reduces duplication, integration cost, and data chaos.

6.      Data Fabric vs Traditional Utility Data Warehouse

Many utilities already operate enterprise data warehouses and may question why a Data Fabric is needed. Traditional data warehouses typically rely on periodic ETL processes that copy data into centralized repositories for reporting purposes. While effective for historical analytics, they often struggle to support real-time operational use cases, cross-domain intelligence, and AI-driven decision making.

Utility Data Fabric complements and extends the traditional warehouse by introducing semantic consistency, federated data access, active governance, reusable data products, and near real-time integration. Rather than simply storing data, the Data Fabric creates a governed and intelligent data layer that connects operational systems, analytical platforms, and AI services across the enterprise.

In simple terms, a traditional warehouse answers questions about the past, while a Data Fabric enables utilities to act on what is happening now.

Traditional Data Warehouse

  • Periodic ETL
  • Historical reporting
  • Centralized storage
  • Primarily analytics focused

Data Fabric

  • Real-time data access
  • Federation and virtualization
  • Semantic consistency
  • Data products
  • AI consumption
  • Cross-domain intelligence

7.      Common Pitfalls (What Fails in Real Projects):

Pitfall: Attempting to replicate all data instead of federating it. Leverage Datasphere’s ability to virtualize data from SAP S/4HANA and external sources to reduce ETL overhead and latency.

  • Treating the Data Fabric as an IT project: It must be business‑owned and domain‑driven.
  • Overloading the data lake with unstructured noise: Raw data without governance becomes a “data swamp.”
  • No semantic consistency: Different teams define the same metric differently.
  • Ignoring real‑time ingestion: Batch pipelines cannot support modern grid operations.
  • Not designing data products:  Data must be reusable, not one‑off.

The Architecture Principle That Matters Most

Data Fabric is not a database. It is a governed operating model for how utility data is shared, trusted, and consumed across the enterprise.

SAP BTP provides the platform.  The utility provides discipline.

What Success Looks Like

A successful Utility Data Fabric delivers:

  • A single source of truth
  • Real‑time grid + customer visibility
  • Faster regulatory reporting
  • Higher data quality
  • Better AI outcomes
  • Lower integration cost
  • Stronger cybersecurity posture
  • Improved customer experience

This is the foundation for the AI‑enabled utility.

8.      Executive Insight

Historically, utilities integrated applications.

The next generation utility will compete on the quality, trustworthiness, and usability of its data as much as the reliability of its physical infrastructure.

Organizations that establish trusted, governed, and reusable data products will be better positioned to deploy AI, automate operations, accelerate regulatory reporting, and improve customer experience. The competitive advantage will come not from possessing more data, but from creating a data architecture capable of turning data into operational intelligence.

9.      Final Thought

Utilities don’t need more data. They need better architecture for the data they already have.

A Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP creates:

  • A unified data layer
  • A governed semantic foundation
  • A real‑time operational backbone
  • A scalable platform for AI and analytics

In the next decade, utilities that master their data will lead. Those that don’t will struggle with reliability, cost, and customer expectations.

Next in the Series — ATUL Architecture #6

We move into the intelligence layer:

  • AI‑driven grid operations
  • Predictive models for BTP
  • Real‑time decision engines
  • Digital twins
  • Autonomous workflows

This is where Data Fabric becomes the brain of the modern utility.

entry.title: Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights
entry.content:
New quarter. New innovations. New opportunities.

SAP Integration Suite continues to evolve, and every update creates new ways to help customers integrate systems faster, streamline business processes, and accelerate transformation.

Want the highlights without spending hours digging through roadmap updates?

Join our webinar, "Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights". We'll cover the biggest innovations from the past quarter, preview what's ahead, and dive into selected capabilities that every SAP Partner should know.

Whether you work in sales, presales, consulting, or delivery, these insights can help you turn roadmap updates into customer value and stronger engagements.

Don't miss your quarterly roadmap update.

Register now and stay ahead of what's next. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013385.html

entry.title: Introducing zero-copy integration between SAP Business Data Cloud and Google BigQuery
entry.content:
Join our SAP Partner session focused on SAP Business Data Cloud Connect for Google BigQuery.

SAP and Google Cloud now work together to enable bidirectional, zero-copy data movement between SAP systems and BigQuery. This means teams can use trusted SAP data together with Google Cloud analytics and AI services in a single flow, without duplication steps slowing things down.

In this session, we will walk through how SAP Business Data Cloud Connect for BigQuery is structured, how data products are shared securely in both directions, and how you can position this value in customer conversations. We will also go through pricing, key architecture elements, and a live demo that shows the integration in action.

You will leave with clear talking points, practical partner guidance, and ready-to-use resources for customer engagements across data and analytics scenarios. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014587.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPBusinessDataCloud #GoogleCloud #DataAnalytics

entry.title: Code Connect 2026: Final Info Before We Kick Off!
entry.content:
Code Connect 2026 is just around the corner! From July 13–16, 2026, the SAP developer community will gather in St. Leon-Rot, Germany – onsite and online.

Here’s everything you need to know before we get started.

 

 

We Are Fully Booked!

All onsite tickets are fully booked - thank you for the amazing interest!

If you couldn’t secure a ticket:

  • Join the waiting lists on Code Connect 2026 in case seats become available.
  • Follow selected sessions via live stream or Microsoft Teams link.

Even if you're not onsite, you can still be part of Code Connect. Selected sessions will be streamed live, and recordings of some sessions will be available afterwards. Check back soon for details.

 

 

 

UI5con (July 14)

  • Main stage sessions will be live streamed on YouTube.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are streamed.

 

 

re>≡CAP (July 15)

  • Main stage sessions will be broadcasted.
  • Some sidetracks (W1/W2) can be attended via Microsoft Teams link.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are broadcasted or available via Microsoft Teams link.

 

 

HANA Tech Con (July 16)

  • The links will be announced on the event day.

 

The Agendas Are Live

The full program is already available - time to plan your schedule!

Check-In opens at 8.00 AM every day. Please bring the QR code with you that you can find in your ticket.

July 13 – Code Jams & Community Meetup

  • Hands-on Code Jams:
    • OpenUI5
    • CAP
    • AI Agents
  • Informal pre-event meetup (no registration needed):
    Ihle Besen, Höfe am Sträßel 3, 69231 Rauenberg
    5:00 PM CEST

July 14–16 – Main Conference Days

Explore all agendas:

  • July 14: UI5con
  • July 15: re>≡CAP
  • July 16: HANA Tech Con

On July 14 also the HANA AI CodeJam takes place.

 

Location

Code Connect takes place at:

SAP-Allee 27
68789 St. Leon-Rot, Germany

Find detailed information on directions, parking, and public transport here.

 

Final Tips

  • Bookmark your favorite sessions in advance
  • Arrive early for popular sessions (rooms fill quickly!)
  • Bring your laptop for Code Jams and hands-on sessions/ workshops
  • Don’t miss the networking moments - some of the best conversations happen in between sessions
  • Follow the agendas closely for last-minute updates

 

See You Soon!

We’re looking forward to an inspiring week of learning, sharing, and connecting.

See you at Code Connect 2026!

entry.title: Update on SAP Fioneer Extensions for FS-CD and ICM
entry.content:
Innovations for Insurance Industry from SAP Fioneer

Overview about Fioneer Commissions and Payments Extensions 

Fioneer Commissions and Payments Extensions (FCPE) support integration with external systems to execute business processes such as broker settlement or real-time payments. Furthermore, it supports a "separation of concerns," meaning that, for example, a business process running in an upstream/external system doesn't have to worry about which dependent processes need to be started in a downstream system. It acts as an integration component to streamline common integration challenges to process broker settlements, outgoing instant payments, or business transactions from insurance core solutions.

The suite is available as Fioneer Payments Extensions for FS-CD customers or as Fioneer Commissions Extensions for ICM customers. It bundles current and future innovations from SAP Fioneer for insurance industry such as

  • Settlement Hub
  • Real-Time Payment
  • Business Process Integration

Settlement Hub

Settlement Hub is a feature for SAP Collections and Disbursements (FS-CD) and provides a holistic solution to manage all relevant aspects of broker collections. 

Fiori App Settlement Hub

It offers a web service that is compatible with standards like BiPRO and a Fiori application that allows users to manage all settlements and clarifications. Furthermore it enables the integration of an ISML model to reconcial policy data, settled premiums, commissions etc. with open items in FS-CD.

Real-Time Payment

With Real-Time Payment, you can manage outgoing payments in real time. 

You can view the real-time payments in the Fiori App Instant Payments. The app allows you to select open outgoing payments and to release them for real-time payment. You can change the payment method, the recipient and the bank account before you release the payment. For example, you can pay a commission to a broker.

Instant Payment App

The underlying services are also integrated into FS-CM for instant claim payments.

Business Process Integration

Business Process Integration (BPI) is an feature that is used for the functional integration and harmonization of processes. It supports a “separation of concerns”, whereby a main business process that is executed in an upstream system doesn’t need to care about which dependent processes need to be started in a downstream system.

Architecture BPI

 

It relies on the usage of Integration Suite to connect with multiple insurance core systems.

Further Information

  • Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISML)
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP )

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration

Part 2 of a 4-part beginner series on SAP Event Mesh.

A quick recap

In Part 1, we covered the "why" and the "what" — Event-Driven Architecture, the publish-subscribe model, and the three building blocks of SAP Event Mesh: topics, queues, and namespaces. We then provisioned an Event Mesh instance on SAP BTP (region AP11), created queues for each consumer system, subscribed those queues to topics, and generated a service key holding our connection credentials.

👉If you haven't read it yet, start with Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics & Queues — this post builds directly on that setup. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/bc-p/14428494#M179525 

So we have a working Event Mesh instance. But how do we actually send a message into it, and how do we get SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) to publish and consume events? That's what we'll do here.

We'll work in two stages:

  1. Validate the setup with Postman — prove that authentication works and that we can publish a message before we touch CPI.
  2. Build the CPI iFlows — one to publish events over HTTP, and one to consume them over AMQP.

Here's the picture we're building toward:

 

 

Let's get started.

3. Testing with Postman

Why test with Postman first? Because if something goes wrong later in CPI, we want to know the Event Mesh side is already working. Postman lets us isolate the problem. It's a habit worth building.

Step 1 — Configure OAuth2 authentication

Open Postman, create a new request, and go to the Authorization tab. We'll set it up like this:

Field Value
Auth TypeOAuth 2.0
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://integrationsuite-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Scope(leave empty)
Client AuthenticationSend client credentials in body

 

 

Now here's the single most common mistake people make, and it's worth pausing on:

⚠️Watch out: The Event Mesh Client ID contains special characters such as | and !. If we send the credentials as a Basic Auth header, those characters get corrupted during encoding, and we get a confusing invalid_client: Bad credentials error — even though our credentials are perfectly correct. The fix is to choose "Send client credentials in body." Always.

Click Get New Access Token. If everything is correct, Postman returns a token. We're authenticated. 🎉

Step 2 — Publish a message to a topic

Now let's send our first event. We use the messaging REST endpoint from the service key:

 
 
POST https://integrationsuite{}.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com     /messagingrest/v1/topics/{encoded-topic-name}/messages

Notice {encoded-topic-name}. This is where beginners lose an hour, so let's be careful.

The URL-encoding rule:

⚠️Every / in the topic name must be encoded as %2F — not %2. The trailing F is required. Leave it off and you'll get a 404 or, worse, silent wrong-topic routing.

So our topic name transforms like this:

  Value
Topic name (plain)S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
Topic name (URL-encoded)S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster

Which gives us the full URL:

 
 
https://integrationsuite {}-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages

Next, set the headers:

Header Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

A quick note on x-qos (quality of service): 0 means at most once (fire and forget — fast, but no delivery guarantee), and 1 means at least once (guaranteed delivery). For our test, 0 is fine.

Finally, the request body — a simple CloudEvents-style payload:

 
 
json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "S4HC.Testing.Masterdata.Shopify.CustomerMaster",
  "source": "/S4HC/Testing/Masterdata",
  "data": {
    "CustomerID": "C001",
    "Name": "Test Customer"
  }
}

 

Hit Send. And now for the part that surprises everyone:

✅A successful publish returns HTTP 204 No Content. There's no response body, and that's correct. It does not mean something went wrong — it means the message was accepted and is now sitting in the queue, waiting for a consumer.

Step 3 — Verify the message arrived

Let's confirm. Go to the Event Mesh UI → Queues, click the queue you subscribed (for example S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify), and check the message count — it should have gone up by one.

 

That's it — we've published an event to Event Mesh and confirmed it landed in the right queue, all without touching CPI yet. Now we know the foundation is solid.

4. CPI iFlow Configuration

Time to bring CPI into the picture. We'll set up the shared credential once, then build the publish and consume sides.

4.1 Create the OAuth2 credential in Security Material

Both of our iFlows will authenticate the same way, so we create the credential once and reuse it.

In CPI, go to Monitor → Security Material → Add, and select OAuth2 Client Credentials:

Field Value
Credential NameEventMeshPOC (remember this name — we'll reference it in the adapters)
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Token Service URLhttps://integration-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Send BodyYes

 

⚠️Same gotcha as Postman: make sure Send Body is set to Yes. The special characters in the Client ID will break Basic Auth.

4.2 Publishing messages — use the HTTP adapter

Here's an important design decision, and it catches a lot of people:

⚠️Known CPI behaviour: The AMQP adapter automatically lowercases topic and queue names. So CustomerMaster silently becomes customermaster, the topic doesn't match, and we get amqp:not-allowed (error 220041). To publish, we sidestep this entirely by using the HTTP Receiver adapter instead, which preserves the exact case.

In your publisher iFlow, add an HTTP Receiver adapter:

Connection tab

Field Value
URLhttps://enterprise-messaging-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages
MethodPOST
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC
Content Typeapplication/json

Then, just before the HTTP adapter, add a Content Modifier step to set the required headers:

Header Name Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

 

That's the publish side done. When this iFlow runs, it POSTs the payload to the topic exactly the way our Postman test did — and we already know that works.

4.3 Consuming messages — use the AMQP adapter

When CPI is the consumer (the subscriber listening for events), we use the AMQP Sender adapter. And here's the key: we consume from a Queue, not a Topic — which conveniently avoids the lowercase issue we hit on the publish side.

AMQP Connection tab

Field Value
Hostenterprise-messaging-messaging-gateway.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com
Port443
Path/protocols/amqp10ws
Proxy TypeInternet
Connect with TLSChecked
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC

⚠️The Host field should contain only the hostname — no wss:// prefix. The protocol path goes in the Path field separately (/protocols/amqp10ws). Mixing them up gives errors like wss: Name or service not known or a failed WebSocket handshake.

AMQP Processing tab

Field Value
Destination TypeQueue
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
DeliveryPersistent

 

✅Tip: Using Queue as the destination type (rather than Topic) on the consume side keeps the exact name intact. Reading from a queue does not have the lowercase problem that publishing to a topic does.

Putting it together

Let's step back and look at what we've built:

  • We validated authentication and publishing with Postman, so we knew the Event Mesh side worked before writing a single iFlow.
  • On the publish side, our CPI iFlow uses the HTTP Receiver adapter to POST events to a topic — preserving case and avoiding the AMQP lowercase trap.
  • On the consume side, a second iFlow uses the AMQP Sender adapter pointed at a Queue to pull events as they arrive.
  • Both adapters share a single OAuth2 Client-Credentials credential, sent in the body.

The two recurring themes — case sensitivity and credentials in the body — are exactly the things that cause the most head-scratching for beginners, so keep them front of mind.

What's next

We now have a complete round trip: events flowing from CPI into Event Mesh and back out again. But the first time you wire this up, something will break — that's normal, and it's where real learning happens.

In Part 3 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll go through the exact errors you're most likely to hit (invalid_client, amqp:not-allowed, the %2F trap, WebSocket handshake failures), what causes each one, and how to fix it — plus a one-page cheat sheet of every URL and value you need.

If this helped, a like or a comment is always appreciated — and feel free to share what you're integrating with Event Mesh. See you in Part 3!

 

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, AMQP, OAuth2, Postman, SAP BTP, Event-Driven Architecture

entry.title: Lernen verbindet – Entdecke unsere 🇩🇪 deutschsprachigen 🇩🇪 Live Sessions in Q3
